coredata.dc:title: Thermal runaway evolution of a 4S4P lithium-ion battery pack induced by both overcharging and unilateral preheating
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Kuo
Zhang, Jianqi
Chang, Chongye
Zhang, Yue
Yan, Ke
Sun, Haoshi
Ouyang, Dongxu
Qian, Xinming
coredata.dc:description: To clarify the thermal runaway characteristics of lithium-ion battery pack, this study has established a thermal runaway experimental platform based on actual power battery pack. A 4 in series and 4 in parallel battery pack was assembled using 86 Ah lithium iron phosphate batteries, and the experiment of thermal runaway induced by overcharging and unilateral preheating was carried out. The behavior and characteristics including the temperature change characteristics of each cell, the heat generated and transfer paths during thermal runaway propagation, the voltage changes of each serial module and the total voltage, flame evolution behavior, gas generation characteristics, debris, and mass loss were investigated. The research results show that module 1 was the first to experience thermal runaway due to preheating. The redistributed current caused the batteries in the remaining modules to rapidly generate heat. Subsequently, the heat transfer from module 1 triggered thermal runaway in modules 2, 3, and 4 in sequence. The entire flame combustion process lasted for 38 min, with the maximum temperature reaching 937.1 °C, resulting in thermal runaway in all batteries. The sequence of thermal runaway has been clarified, with the flame generated by the ignition of the battery casing, connecting tabs, and combustible gases emitted from the batteries serving as the primary paths for heat transfer and thermal radiation. The experimental results provide valuable insights into the thermal engineering issues of large-scale lithium-ion battery pack.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Module-level thermal runaway
Overcharge
Heat propagation

Analysis of thermal runaway mechanism of battery pack
Module voltage and battery box space temperature
Thermal runaway gas production and mass loss

Temperature and voltage changes of four series battery modules.
Temperature rise rate curve of four series battery modules.
TR transmission path of the battery pack.

TR evolution in front and side shots.
Probability diagram of TR flame morphology of battery pack.
Real-time concentration of TR gas in battery pack.
TR mass loss and debris of the battery pack.
Key parameters of TR of a single cell.

head.ce:title: Thermal runaway evolution of a 4S4P lithium-ion battery pack induced by both overcharging and unilateral preheating

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Explosion Science and Safety Protection, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Explosion Science and Safety Protection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Safety Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Safety Science and Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 210009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To clarify the thermal runaway characteristics of lithium-ion battery pack, this study has established a thermal runaway experimental platform based on actual power battery pack. A 4 in series and 4 in parallel battery pack was assembled using 86 Ah lithium iron phosphate batteries, and the experiment of thermal runaway induced by overcharging and unilateral preheating was carried out. The behavior and characteristics including the temperature change characteristics of each cell, the heat generated and transfer paths during thermal runaway propagation, the voltage changes of each serial module and the total voltage, flame evolution behavior, gas generation characteristics, debris, and mass loss were investigated. The research results show that module 1 was the first to experience thermal runaway due to preheating. The redistributed current caused the batteries in the remaining modules to rapidly generate heat. Subsequently, the heat transfer from module 1 triggered thermal runaway in modules 2, 3, and 4 in sequence. The entire flame combustion process lasted for 38 min, with the maximum temperature reaching 937.1 °C, resulting in thermal runaway in all batteries. The sequence of thermal runaway has been clarified, with the flame generated by the ignition of the battery casing, connecting tabs, and combustible gases emitted from the batteries serving as the primary paths for heat transfer and thermal radiation. The experimental results provide valuable insights into the thermal engineering issues of large-scale lithium-ion battery pack.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

row.entry: total heat generated by a battery due to overcharging (J)
thermal conductivity resistance of two adjacent temperature nodes (W·K−1)
temperature difference between two adjacent temperature nodes (K)
heat transfer distance of the corresponding heat transfer path (m)
thermal conductivity resistance of the corresponding heat transfer path (W·m−1·K−1)
heat flow through area of the corresponding heat transfer path (m2)
specific heat capacity of the battery J/(g·K)

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have developed rapidly due to their advantages such as high energy density, long cycle life, environmental friendliness, and have become widely used as power sources for electric vehicles (EVs) and energy storage stations (ESS) [1]. However, in recent years, safety issues caused by thermal runaway (TR) in LIBs have occurred frequently, their safety and reliability have yet to be fully resolved. Fires and explosions caused by battery TR and its propagation are not uncommon, resulting in significant property damage and personal injury. This directly hinders the large-scale popularization of EVs and ESS. For example, in March 2017, a Tesla electric car caught fire and was destroyed while charging at a supercharger station in Shanghai, China. In August 2018, a lithium iron phosphate (LFP) ESS caught fire due to an overcharge caused by a reversed power line during construction and debugging in Jiangsu, China. In April 2022, a fire occurred at a storage facility within the Salt River Substation in Arizona, USA, which lasted for several days with smoke continuously emitting [2]. These accidents can be attributed to mechanical abuse, electrical abuse, and thermal abuse of the batteries. The unique challenges posed by energy storage facilities in the realms of fire prevention and suppression have been brought into sharper focus.

TR is the core of safety issues surrounding LIBs. Numerous researchers have conducted extensive studies, primarily focusing on the inherent safety of LIBs (such as increasing the melting temperature of separators [3], selecting non-flammable electrolytes [4,5], using cathodes with high thermal stability [6], and employing lithium-dendrite-free anodes [7]) as well as safety prevention and control measures (using phase change materials [8,9], optimizing battery structures [10], employing air or liquid cooling techniques [11], and efficient fire extinguishing technology [12–14]). Although these measures can delay the TR process of LIBs, the issue still cannot be fundamentally solved. Battery TR refers to a phenomenon where the heat inside the battery cannot be effectively dissipated, resulting in an uncontrollable rise in temperature [15]. This, in turn, triggers a series of chain reactions that ultimately lead to the occurrence of battery fires and explosions [16]. TR in a single battery mainly involves a chain reaction process including the decomposition of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI), reaction between the anode and electrolyte, melting of the separator, decomposition of the electrolyte, decomposition of the cathode, decomposition of the binder, and combustion of the electrolyte [17]. To meet the voltage and current demands in practical applications, batteries are often connected in series or parallel to form the required pack. The TR in a single battery within a pack is sufficient to trigger TR in adjacent batteries, leading to module-level failures. During the TR process, the battery pack releases a significant amount of heat in a short period of time, and the propagation of this heat can be influenced by various factors such as abuse mode, pack structure, battery casing shape and material, connection method, and heat dissipation method [18]. Hence, additional research is imperative to thoroughly examine the propagation behavior of TR in battery pack.

A significant amount of research has been focused on the TR propagation in LIB pack. Ouyang et al. [19] conducted an experimental study on the TR propagation in cylindrical LIB pack. The results showed that the higher the state of charge (SOC) of the battery, the faster the thermal diffusion and the higher the surface temperature of the battery. There is a significant domino effect during the TR propagation of the battery pack. Hu et al. [20] studied the impact of different charging currents and SOC on the TR propagation of LIB pack under charging conditions. The results showed that as the charging current increases, the time for battery TR propagation is significantly shortened, and a high charging rate accelerates the TR propagation. Zhong et al. [21] analyzed the risk of TR propagation in 18650 LIBs, and found that it took 240–280 s for the propagation to occur between different layers of the battery. Cheng et al. [22] conducted an experiment on the overheating abuse of a 37 Ah 12S1P prismatic battery pack. The results showed that the thermal abuse process of the battery pack could be divided into three stages: TR initiation, propagation, and termination. Typical phenomenon of TR include gas ejection, flame ejection, and flame combustion. Furthermore, a thermal propagation coefficient was proposed to assess the ability of TR propagation. Song et al. [23] systematically studied the TR propagation behavior of 280 Ah LFP batteries with different SOCs. They revealed the distribution of energy flow during the propagation process of large-sized batteries, finding that only batteries with 100 % SOC exhibited TR propagation. Among the energy released, 75 % was used to heat the battery itself, while 20 % was used for ejection. Feng et al. [24] triggered the propagation of TR by puncturing the first battery, and clarifying the mechanism of TR propagation in prismatic batteries. There are relatively few experimental studies on the TR of large LIB pack induced by overcharging. Sun et al. [25] established a realistic prefabricated cabin environment for ESS and conducted a preliminary investigation on the TR process of LFP battery pack under two different overcharging conditions: continuous overcharging and overcharging until the safety valve opens. The experimental results revealed that terminating the overcharging process once the safety valve is activated can effectively curb the progression of TR in the battery. Currently, research on TR at the module-level of LIBs primarily focuses on cylindrical LIBs, with the triggering methods primarily centering around thermal abuse and mechanical abuse. Due to high costs, difficulties, and risks, there has been limited in-depth experimental research on TR in large LIB pack triggered by overcharging.

This study centers on a 4S4P LIB pack as its research subject. By conducting unilateral preheating and overall overcharging experiments on the battery pack, it investigates the progression of TR in module-level LIBs, the sequence of TR propagation, the primary heat flow transmission pathways, voltage fluctuations, flame and gas production characteristics, and mass loss. The research findings offer valuable insights for the safe design of module-level LIBs.

The battery samples used in this study are 86 Ah LFP prismatic batteries (CATL) commonly used in EVs and ESS. Detailed parameters of individual batteries are listed in Table 1 . To avoid the inconsistency in the SOC of individual battery that may arise from directly charging the module, a battery charge and discharge tester (5 V, 100 A, YKY TECH) was used to charge each cell to 100 % SOC before the experiment. Each cell is first discharged at a constant current of 43 A to 2.5 V (minimum cut-off voltage) to release any residual energy. Then, it is charged at a constant current and voltage to 3.65 V (maximum cut-off voltage) until the charging current drops to 4.3 A. The fully charged battery is then left for 12 h to ensure stability.

The entire experimental system is shown in Fig. 1 (a). The battery pack consists of 16 individual batteries. Using connecting copper tabs, four batteries are paralleled into one module and then four such modules are connected in series to form a 4S4P battery pack (with a rated voltage of 12.8 V, a rated capacity of 1376 Ah, and a rated power of 4.4 kWh). After assembly, the battery pack is placed in a battery box equipped with insulating mica sheets to reduce heat loss. Given that LFP batteries do not spontaneously ignite after TR, a heating plate with a power of 200 W is placed on the outer side of the 1–4 cell in the pack to induce TR first. Additionally, an electric igniter is positioned 20 cm above the heating plate. The layout is shown in Fig. 1(b). The experiment was conducted in an explosion chamber, with the experimental setup arranged as shown in Fig. 1(c). A hole was made on the side of the battery box for gas collection. With the assistance of a vacuum pump, the real-time concentrations of gases (CO, CO2, H2) produced during TR of LIBs were monitored. K-type thermocouples with a diameter of 1 mm were placed on the surface of the batteries, and Teflon tape was used to attach the thermocouples to the center of the cell surface to measure the temperature of each cell. The temperature data was transmitted to the computer through a data acquisition module (ART, DAM-3038). Thermocouples were also placed on the four sides of the battery box, and the locations are shown in Fig. 1(d). The thermocouples on the top were used to measure the flame temperature, approximately 30 cm away from the battery surface. The experimental phenomena were recorded by a 60 Hz camera (SONY, HDR-CX405), placed in front of and to the left of the battery pack. Additionally, infrared thermography (FLTR, A300) was used to capture the flame. The battery was overcharged using a pack test system (60 V, 200 A, YKY TECH), and the voltage of each series module and the total voltage were monitored.

Experimental Procedure: The fully charged battery pack is first left to stand for 3 min (start other detection devices), followed by overcharging at a rate of 0.5C (172 A) for 24 min before being left to stand again. Considering that it is difficult to determine which part will suffer TR first during the overcharge of the battery pack, in this study, Module 1 was preheated for 10 min (with a power of 200 W) at the same time as the overcharge was initiated, aiming to induce thermal runaway in the battery of Module 1 first. In addition, the electric igniter was positioned above Module 1, and it would be continuously activated once the safety valve was opened and the electrolyte vapor eject.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface temperatures of the battery module during thermal runaway
ce:section.ce:para: The entire TR process of the battery lasted nearly 38 min, with the four serially connected battery modules experiencing the opening of the safety valve, gas ejection, flame emission, and stable combustion in sequence. All 16 cells underwent TR. Fig. 2 presents the voltage and temperature change curves of the four serially connected battery modules during the overcharge process. The curves are divided into three stages: Stage a, Stage b, and Stage c, representing the battery overcharge stage, TR propagation stage, and natural cooling stage, respectively.

Fig. 2(a) shows the temperature and voltage curves of the serially connected Module 1. Due to the preheating effect of the heating plate, the temperature of the 1–4 cell increases significantly. Once the preheating terminates, the battery temperature begins to decrease. Under the combined effect of heating and overcharging, the 1–4 cell experiences TR first, and the generated heat is transferred to the 1–3 cell. The time interval between the TR of the two batteries is 126 s, with the 1–2 and 1-1 cells experiencing TR last. Based on Ohm's law and the characteristics of parallel circuits [26], in a parallel circuit, the voltage across each branch is equal. The branch with a smaller resistance offers the least resistance to the flow of current, resulting in a larger current flow in that branch. After the TR of 1–3 and 1–4 cells, the overcharge process was still ongoing. Due to the occurrence of severe internal short circuits, the resistance approached 0 Ω, preventing the current from flowing through the loads of 1-2and 1-1 cells. Therefore, the TR of 1-1 cell and 1–2 cell was triggered by heat conduction. The whole stage b lasts 1855 s Fig. 2(b) shows the temperature and voltage curves of the second serially connected module. Due to the heat transfer from the TR of the first module, 2-2, 2–3, and 2–4 cells began to exhibit significant temperature rises during stage a. At 1516 s, battery 2-2 cell was the first to undergo TR, subsequently triggering the TR of battery 2–3 cell. The heat then transferred to battery 2-1 cell, and the entire stage b lasted for 763 s. Affected by the flames, some of the thermocouples exhibited violent fluctuations. During stage c, the natural cooling phase began, and the temperature curves started to decline gradually. Fig. 2(c) depicts the temperature and voltage curves of the third serially connected module during TR. Throughout stage a, the thermocouples did not exhibit significant temperature rises. This was due to the fact that as the overcharge process continued, the heat generated by the side reactions caused by overcharging was much greater than the heat transferred from TR in other modules [27]. Meanwhile, because 3-2 and 3-3 cells were located at the center of the module, heat dissipation was poor. Coupled with the influence of TR in the second module, 3-2 and 3-3 cells underwent TR first. The entire stage b lasted for 1023 s Fig. 4(d) presents the temperature and voltage curves of the fourth serially connected module during TR. The heat generated by the side reactions of battery overcharging gradually replaced the heat absorbed from the surrounding environment, becoming the main source of heat accumulation. The entire stage b lasted for 1066 s. The key parameters of TR time and temperature for each cell are listed in Table 2 .

TR of LIBs refers to a phenomenon where the internal heat of the battery continuously accumulates under abusive conditions, leading to an uncontrollable increase in battery temperature due to an exothermic chain reaction. Based on the experimental phenomena and temperature rate curves, this paper defines TR as the point when the temperature rise rate reaches 2 °C/s [28], and the duration of this condition must be greater than 5 s. The temperature rise rate curve is shown in Fig. 3 (green dashed line: stopping overcharging). The sequence of TR occurrence is clarified: 1–4→1-3→2-2→3-2→3-3→2-3→4-3→4-2→4-1→4-4→3–4→2-4→2-1→3-1→1–2→1-1. A detailed analysis on the propagation path please refers to Section 3.2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of thermal runaway mechanism of battery pack
ce:section.ce:para:
Since 1–4 cell are exposed to the heating plate, the heat they receive is represented by Eq (1).
(1)
Q
h e a t e r
= α · S ·
P
h e a t e r
where, α is the radiation absorption coefficient; S is the heating area, m2; P
heater is radiative heat flux.

The heat generated during the propagation of TR mainly originates from the chemical reactions inside the battery triggered by overcharging. The total heat generated by a battery due to overcharging, Q
total , is mainly composed of the heat generated by side reactions, Q
side , the released electrical energy, Q
ele , the chemical energy, Q
chem , and the dissipated heat, Q
p (as shown in Eq (2)). Each component of the heat can be calculated using the heat calculation Eq. (3) [29,30].
(2)
Q
t o t a l
=
Q
s i d e
+
Q
e l e
+
Q
c h e m
+
Q p
(3)
Q = M · C p · ∫ d T
where, C
p is the specific heat capacity of the battery, J/(g·K); M is the battery mass, 2100 g.

The heat lost from a thermal runaway battery through thermal convection
Q cov
and thermal radiation
Q
r a d
can be calculated using Eqs. (4) and (5), respectively [31].
(4)
Q
c o v
= h A
(
T b
−
T
a i r
)
(5)
Q
r a d
= ε σ
(
T b 4
−
T
a i r
4
)
where, h is the convective heat transfer coefficient, J/(m2·K), A is the battery surface area, m2; T
b is the battery temperature, K; T
air is the air temperature, K, ε is the surface emissivity.

There exists a certain degree of inconsistency among individual batteries, resulting in different degrees of overcharge and varying extents of side reactions within the battery module. Heat is transferred to adjacent batteries through thermal conduction, convection, and radiation, further intensifying heat generation and diffusion. The heat propagation of TR mainly occurs through three main paths [24,32]: first, through the battery casing; secondly, through the terminal posts and connecting tabs that connect the batteries; and finally, through the heat radiation generated by the flames that ignite the combustible gases produced by the batteries, which also impart a certain amount of heat to adjacent batteries. The heat flux, q, for each heat transfer path can be calculated using the appropriate Eq. (6).
(6)
q =
Δ T
R
where, R is the thermal conductivity resistance of two adjacent temperature nodes, W·K−1, and ΔT is the temperature difference between two adjacent temperature nodes, K. The thermal conductivity resistance R can be calculated by the following Eq. (7).
(7)
R =
δ
λ ⋅ A
where, δ is the heat transfer distance of the corresponding heat transfer path, m, λ is the thermal conductivity resistance of the corresponding heat transfer path, W·m−1·K−1, and A is the heat flow through area of the corresponding heat transfer path, m2. Finally, the total heat Q
tran transferred by each path can be calculated by Eq. (8).
(8)
Q
t r a n
= ∫ q
( x )
d x

Based on the measured temperatures on the battery surface, an analysis of the heat transfer path during TR propagation was conducted, as shown in Fig. 4 . As the overcharge process continues, the batteries begin to expand, resulting in a decrease in the contact area, A, between them. Simultaneously, the heat dissipation between the batteries improves significantly, leading to a notable extension in the TR trigger time for the batteries on the sides. During the TR process of the entire LIB pack, a domino effect exists, and the propagation of TR can be divided into four processes. Firstly, under the preheating effect of an external heat source, the 1–4 cell experiences TR first, generating flames and heat radiation from its high-temperature casing that acts on the surrounding batteries. The intensity of thermal radiation decreases as the distance increases, therefore, it propagates first to the 1–3 cell. Secondly, as the overcharge continues, the 2-2 and 2–3 cells in the center of the pack, which have difficulty dissipating heat, subsequently undergo TR, followed by the similarly centered 3-2 and 3-3 cells, and then to 4-2 and 4-3 cells. Subsequently, the heat generated by the thermally runaway batteries in the center spreads to the surrounding batteries, causing TR in the upper and lower cells (such as 1-1, 2-1, 2–4, 3-1, 3–4, 4-1, 4-4). Finally, 1-1 and 1–2 cells undergo TR last, as they have not undergone complete overcharge.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Module voltage and battery box space temperature
ce:section.ce:para:
In Fig. 5 , the curves represent the total voltage of the pack and the maximum voltage difference among each series of modules. The total voltage curve can be divided into six stages:
Stage Ⅰ: The voltage rapidly rises to 18.6 V. Although the individual cell has been charged to 100 % SOC, there is still some space left in the cathode and anode. At this point, Li+ can still be rapidly de-intercalated from the cathode and intercalated into the anode [33].
Stage Ⅱ: The voltage steadily rises to the voltage platform (V
p ) of 22.9 V. As the Li+ is excessively de-intercalated from the cathode of the battery and the capacity of the anode graphite is limited, it becomes difficult to intercalated additional Li [34]. Excess Li+ deposit on the anode, making it difficult for the voltage to rise rapidly, resulting in a steady increase to the V
p
.
Stage Ⅲ: The voltage remains constant at the voltage plateau for nearly 200 s. The emergence of the voltage plateau may be due to the formation of large areas of lithium dendrites within the battery pack, as well as the collapse of the cathode material, which prevents the continuous de-intercalated of Li+ [35].
Stage Ⅳ: After experiencing a stable voltage plateau, the battery pack begins to enter a voltage drop zone. The formation of a large number of lithium dendrites on the anode punctures the separator, resulting in internal short circuits within the battery and a decrease in voltage [36]. Simultaneously, due to the inconsistency in the formation of internal short circuits among various battery modules, the voltage difference also begins to aggravate. Additionally, the significant expansion of the battery increases the distance between the positive and negative electrodes, leading to an increase in internal resistance within the battery, which also contributes to the decrease in voltage.
Stage Ⅴ: After passing through the voltage drop zone, overcharging continues. At this point, the overcharging process has far exceeded the internal short circuit, and the battery has entered a state of deep overcharge. The voltage rises sharply, reaching V
max of 45.5 V. A maximum voltage difference of 14.4 V also appears among the various series modules. This indicates that TR has become inevitable.
Stage Ⅵ: The voltage experiences a sharp drop as the ohmic internal resistance of the battery becomes disordered. Under the dual effects of internal side reactions and high temperatures within the battery, the separator collapses completely, and the battery enters a full short-circuit state, causing the voltage to plummet.

The temperature changes at different locations within a space are crucial for understanding heat transfer, dissipation, and thermal equilibrium. Fig. 6 (a) shows the temperature change curves of the bottom, left side, and right side of the battery module box. Due to the difficulty in dissipating heat from the lower part of the battery, the temperature of the lower section starts to rise first. Around 1210 s, the left-side thermocouple experiences a significant temperature increase as the first module experiences TR. At around 1490 s, the right-side thermocouple of the battery box begins to show a temperature increase. There is a difference of approximately 280 s between the onset of temperature increases on the left and right sides. The duration of the temperature rise on the left side is the longest, lasting for 2199 s, while the duration on the right side is 1281 s. Additionally, the peak temperature on the left side, which is 219.4 °C, is higher than that on the right side, which is 159.4 °C. This confirms that the TR on the left side is more intense and lasts longer due to the additional application of the heating plate. The maximum temperature at the bottom reaches 299.2 °C, with a heat dissipation rate of −0.016 °C/s, indicating that the heat accumulated from the TR of the battery module is difficult to dissipate from the bottom. Fig. 6(b) shows the flame temperature above the battery box. High-temperature smoke and heat are released above the battery module, and the entire combustion process lasts approximately 2223 s. It can be observed that there are multiple fluctuations and peaks in the flame propagation process, with the highest temperature reaching 937.1 °C. This indicates a certain degree of instability in the flame during the TR of the entire module. The ejection flame from the high-pressure gas spraying out of the battery's safety valve is usually highly dynamic and unstable.

The flame caused by the TR of a battery can be seen as an external manifestation of the side reactions inside the battery. The increase in flame length and acceleration of the flame represent the increase in gas production inside the battery, which also implies the occurrence of more side reactions. The video process recorded by the camera is shown in Fig. 7 . After the overcharging starts, the battery inside the pack experiences significant expansion and deformation. Due to the preheating of 1–4 cell, the internal pressure inside battery further increased, causing the safety valves to open first. At 1088 s, the electrolyte vapor was ejected in an inverted cone shape. Once the electrolyte vapor and flammable gas accumulated to a combustible concentration, the igniter was activated at 1120 s, triggering intense combustion in an instant. At this point, the temperature of 1–4 cell rapidly increases and begin to undergo TR, transitioning into a jet flame. With the transfer of heat from module 1 and the continuous overcharging of the batteries, TR and ejection flames began to occur in modules 2–4 in sequence from 1149 s to 1605 s. By 1649 s, most of the safety valves in the entire battery pack had opened, and the flame transitioned to stable combustion with a blue hue at the bottom, which may indicate a high concentration of methane and carbon monoxide released from the batteries [22]. Afterwards, TR of the batteries within the pack occurred frequently. At 3284 s, the flame and smoke alternated in eruption, and the concentration of flammable gas generated by the batteries began to decrease, causing the flame began to attenuate. As the oxygen content in the explosion chamber was depleted, high-speed ejected gas simultaneously formed turbulence around the battery safety valves. As a result, the flame gradually weakened and began to undergo incomplete combustion, turning red and accompanied by a certain amount of smoke release. Finally, at 3343 s, the flame completely extinguished, and the entire combustion process lasted for 2223 s.

The morphology of the flame was determined through image binarization [37,38]. Using MATLAB software, each frame of infrared images captured by the infrared thermal imager was converted into a binary image. The software calculated the binary images by combining the number of pixel points where the ejected flame appeared at each point in each frame of the image, ultimately generating a probability matrix. Fig. 8 depicts the possibility of the occurrence of ejection flames at specific locations during the entire TR process of a LIB pack. From the flame morphology during the TR of the battery pack, the entire combustion flame exhibits a semi-circular shape. The high-temperature bracket has caused some interference with the flame morphology. The height and area of the flame are crucial for assessing the severity and spread speed of the fire. Calculations yield an average flame height of approximately 64 cm and an average flame area of 1765 cm2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal runaway gas production and mass loss
ce:section.ce:para: The main gases generated during the TR of LFP batteries triggered by overcharging are H2, CO, CO2, and some CxHy. The specific content of these gases is related to factors such as battery component and charging rate [34]. The gas concentration changed during the experiment is shown in Fig. 9 . Since the igniter ignited and stably burned the combustible gas injected after the safety valve of the battery was opened, the CO2 concentration steadily increased and reached the first peak of 1040 ppm in the early stage, while the concentrations of CO and H2 basically remained unchanged during this stage. As the flammable gas mixture burned fully, a large amount of CO2 was released, reaching the second peak of 1303 ppm. Due to the prolonged combustion of the battery, the oxygen content in the explosion chamber significantly decreased, resulting in incomplete combustion of the gas generated by the battery at this point. As the combustion gradually weakened, the release of smoke and gas increased, and the concentrations of CO and H2 reached the first peaks at 60 ppm and 341 ppm, respectively. After reaching the first peak, the gas concentrations decreased to a certain extent. This was because the flame completely extinguished and was replaced by a large release of smoke and gas, resulting in the second concentration peaks of 113 ppm and 637 ppm for CO and H2, respectively. Complete combustion leads to a decrease in the content of flammable gases. If incomplete combustion occurs, a large amount of flammable gas will be released. Once the concentration of flammable gas reaches its explosion limit and encounters an ignition source, there is a high possibility of deflagration occurring.

Fig. 10 shows the mass loss and debris appearance of the battery module after TR. It can be observed that the battery mass loss gradually decreases from module 1 to module 4, with average mass losses of 487 g, 484 g, 481 g, and 471 g, respectively. The mass loss of the battery primarily depends on the intensity of TR. Since additional heating was applied to module 1, the TR of the battery became more intense, which also meant that the exothermic side reactions inside the battery became more severe, resulting in the ejection of a large amount of gas and solid particles from the battery [32,39]. This also indicates that the heat transferred from module 1 to module 4 gradually dissipated during the entire TR process, resulting in a significant decrease in the intensity of TR. Under the combined effects of heat and overcharging, the 1–4 cell exhibited the most intense ejection of solid particles, accompanied by violent combustion. The debris surface of module 1 is covered with a layer of black substance, making it difficult to see the upper surface of the battery. Compared to module 1, the debris of modules 2, 3, and 4 are clearer, allowing the positions of the connecting tabs and safety valves to be clearly visible.
Some measures to avoid thermal runaway are proposed in this study:

Optimize the thermal management design of the battery module: design an efficient thermal management system focusing on the central area of the battery, the total positive and negative connection area of the battery pack and the bottom area of the battery pack. Ensure battery consistency: Ensure that the performance parameters (such as capacity, internal resistance, voltage, etc.) of all batteries in the battery module are as consistent as possible. Strict monitoring and management: Install temperature sensors, current and voltage sensors to monitor the working status of the battery module in real time. The TR alarm system is established, focusing on the phenomenon of voltage platform and voltage difference between modules, and the appearance of CO2 can also be used as an early warning gas. Strengthen safety protection: set insulation materials between battery modules to reduce the spread of TR between modules. Use LIBs special fire extinguishing agent or fire extinguishing device to quickly extinguish the fire and prevent the spread of the fire caused by TR.

In this study, the TR triggered by overcharge abuse in a 4S4P battery pack consisting of 16 LFP cells with a capacity of 86 Ah was investigated. The temperature change characteristics of each individual cell, the voltage variations within each series module and the overall voltage, flame evolution behavior, gas production characteristics, debris, mass loss, as well as the heat generation and transfer paths during TR propagation were analyzed. The study comprehensively elucidated the TR evolution process of large-scale LIB pack, and the main conclusions are as follows:

Due to the dual effects of overcharging and preheating, TR occurred first in battery module 1. Subsequently, modules 2, 3, and 4 underwent TR in sequence due to the transfer of heat from the TR. Once a battery experiences a severe internal short circuit, the current will redistribute. Based on Ohm's law and the characteristics of parallel circuits, the current flows more significantly in the branch with lower resistance. Consequently, the batteries connected in parallel rely more on heat transfer to trigger TR, rather than TR caused by overcharging. Furthermore, as the overcharge process continues, the influence of external heat on the battery is gradually superseded, and the side reactions caused by overcharge become the dominant factor in heat accumulation.

During the TR of a battery pack induced by overcharging, the total voltage also exhibits a voltage plateau, lasting for nearly 200 s, which can serve as an important parameter for early warning of TR in battery pack. Meanwhile, the voltage difference between various series modules can also serve as an important parameter for early warning of battery pack TR. Once a sharp increase in the maximum voltage difference among the battery modules is observed, it indicates that the battery pack has entered a state of severe internal short circuit. In terms of the spatial temperature within the battery box, it is difficult for the heat generated at the bottom of the battery to dissipate promptly, resulting in a cooling rate of only −0.016 °C/s. Additionally, high-temperature smoke and heat are released above the battery pack. Therefore, special attention should be paid to the heat dissipation at the bottom and the top of the battery pack.

Gas ejection and flame combustion are typical phenomena of TR in battery pack. The entire combustion process lasted for 2223 s, during which jet fires and stable combustion alternated. The color of the flame varied with the ejection intensity, oxygen concentration, and the progress of TR. The results of flame morphology determined through infrared photography and image binarization methods indicate that the flame exhibited a semicircular shape throughout the entire TR process. The average flame height was approximately 64 cm, and the average flame area was 1765 cm2. The full combustion of flammable gases from the battery prevented the generation of CO and H2 in the early stages. However, as the battery flame began to undergo incomplete combustion, the concentration of CO2 began to decrease, while the concentrations of CO and H2 significantly increased. During the entire TR process, the heat transferred from module 1 to module 4 gradually dissipated, resulting in a significant decrease in the intensity of TR and a gradual reduction in cell mass loss.

Kuo Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jianqi Zhang: Methodology, Investigation. Chongye Chang: Investigation. Yue Zhang: Methodology. Ke Yan: Methodology. Haoshi Sun: Investigation. Dongxu Ouyang: Supervision, Project administration. Xinming Qian: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52304228), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2022M711602), the Chunhui Plan of Chinese Ministry of Education (No. HZKY20220135), the Open Project of Guangdong Key Laboratory of Fuel Cell Technology (No. FC202320), and the Open Project of Sichuan Province All-electric Navigation Aircraft Key Technology Engineering Research Center (No. CAFUC202403KF002).
